articles.title: Colon Cancer's Special Germs
articles.summary: Scientists found something special about colon cancer. It has its own unique germs, called a microbial fingerprint. This is different from other cancers. The study looked at over 9,000 cancer patients. They used DNA from tumors to see what germs were inside. Only colon cancer had these special germs. This discovery could help doctors treat colon cancer better. They might use it to diagnose the disease more accurately. The research was done by Dr. Abraham Gihawi and his team. They hope this will improve cancer care. Colon cancer is a common and serious disease. Understanding its germs could lead to new treatments.

articles.title: Artemis II Blasts Off to the Moon
articles.summary: Artemis II is a big rocket that blasted into space with astronauts on board. They are going on a 10-day trip around the Moon. The astronauts won't land on the Moon, but they will fly around it and go farther from Earth than anyone has ever gone before. This mission is part of NASA's Artemis program, which started with an unmanned mission in 2022. The goal is to help humans explore deep space and maybe go to Mars in the future. The BBC is showing live coverage of the launch so people can watch it happen. It's a historic event because it's a step toward new space adventures.

articles.title: Fun Science Stories: Raccoons and Space Sperm
articles.summary: This article shares cool science stories that almost got missed. It talks about raccoons, which are animals that live near people and like to get into trash. Scientists found that raccoons are good at solving puzzles, like opening boxes to get marshmallows. They keep trying even after they get the treat, showing they are curious and smart. Another story is about sperm in space. Scientists tested sperm from humans, mice, and pigs in a machine that acts like zero gravity. They found that sperm get lost and can't find eggs easily in space, but adding a special chemical called progesterone can help. The article also mentions other stories, like finding old writings and folding crepes, but focuses on these two main ones.

articles.title: Supernovae That Leave No Black Holes
articles.summary: Scientists are learning about black holes from space data. Black holes form when big stars explode in supernovae. Some stars are so huge that they explode in a special way called a pair-instability supernova. This explosion is so strong that it destroys the star completely, leaving no black hole behind. Researchers used data from detectors that sense gravitational waves, which are ripples in space from black holes crashing together. They found a gap in the sizes of black holes, meaning some sizes are missing. This gap suggests that pair-instability supernovae happen, stopping black holes from forming above a certain mass, around 45 times the Sun's mass. This helps us understand how stars die and what happens in the universe.

articles.title: A Cute Baby Dinosaur Found in Korea
articles.summary: Scientists in South Korea found a new baby dinosaur hidden inside a rock. They named it Doolysaurus after a famous cartoon dinosaur called Dooly, which is loved by many people in Korea. This is the first new dinosaur species discovered in Korea in 15 years. The dinosaur was about two years old when it died and was as big as a turkey. It might have had soft, fuzzy skin like a little lamb. Scientists used a special machine called a micro-CT scan to see the bones inside the rock without breaking it. This helped them find more parts, like pieces of the skull. The discovery is exciting because it helps us learn more about dinosaurs that lived long ago.

articles.title: NASA's Big Moon Mission: Artemis II Launch Day
articles.summary: NASA is getting ready to launch the Artemis II mission to the Moon. Four astronauts will fly in the Orion spacecraft on a nine-day trip. They will travel far from Earth, go behind the Moon, and come back fast. The launch is from Kennedy Space Center in Florida. This mission is important because it is the first crewed flight of the Artemis program, which aims to send people back to the Moon after many years and maybe go to Mars later. The astronauts have a busy schedule and hope everything goes well. If they cannot launch on the first day, they have other chances later.
